content:
Let the small circle \(C_1\) have radius \(r\) and centre \(I\).  Since \(C_1\) is internally tangent to the big circle \(C_2\) of radius \(9\) at \(A\), we have
\[
OI=9-r.
\]
The point \(P\) on \(C_1\) is such that the line \(OP\) is tangent to \(C_1\), so
\[
OP^2 = OI^2 - r^2 = (9-r)^2 - r^2 =81-18r.
\]
Hence
\[
OP = \sqrt{81-18r}\,.
\]
Parametrize \(OP=d\).  The ray \(OP\) meets the big circle again at \(Q\) with \(OQ=9\), so
\[
PQ =9-d.
\]
The tangent to \(C_1\) at \(A\) is perpendicular to the line of centres \(OI\), hence is a vertical line if we put \(O\) at the origin and \(A\) at \((9,0)\).  Its intersection \(R\) with the line \(OP\) occurs at a distance
\[
OR=\frac{9}{x_P}\,OP,
\]
where \(x_P\) is the \(x\)-coordinate of \(P\).  One finds \(OR-9=\displaystyle\frac{9d}{x_P}-9\).  Imposing
\[
PQ=QR
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
9-d=\frac{9d}{x_P}-9
\]
leads (after some algebra) to the unique nonzero solution
\[
r=4,\quad d=OP=3.
\]
Thus 
\[
\boxed{3}.
\]

\[
PQ =9-d.
